faculty.title: Professor
faculty.research_interests: Marine ecology, conservation biology, fisheries, coral reefs, marine protected areas
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Octavio Aburto‑Oropeza’s research integrates marine ecology and conservation biology to understand and protect coastal and open‑ocean habitats such as mangroves, kelp forests, and coral reefs. He evaluates the effectiveness of marine protected areas, fisheries management, and ecosystem‑based approaches under climate change stressors, including ocean heatwaves and sea‑level rise. His work also quantifies ecosystem services—blue carbon storage, juvenile fish production, and tourism benefits—to inform policy and reduce socioeconomic impacts of natural hazards.

faculty.title: Professor
faculty.research_interests: Physical oceanography, internal waves, ocean mixing, turbulence
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Matthew Alford investigates the physical processes that drive ocean mixing, focusing on internal waves, turbulence, and diapycnal fluxes across a range of marine environments from the Arctic to the South China Sea. His work integrates in‑situ microstructure measurements, distributed optical‑fiber sensing, and moored profiling to quantify turbulent heat and salt fluxes, tidal dissipation, and sediment transport, and he also contributes to the development of internal‑wave mixing parameterizations for climate models. Earlier in his career he explored neurobiological mechanisms of Alzheimer’s disease, studying amyloid‑beta dynamics and apolipoprotein E signaling in animal models.

faculty.research_interests: Marine microbiology, phytoplankton genomics, biogeochemistry, metagenomics
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Andrew Allen investigates marine microbial ecology with an emphasis on phytoplankton genomics, biogeochemistry, and metagenomics. His work integrates field sampling across ocean basins (e.g., Southern Ocean, South Pacific, Indian Ocean) with genome‑resolved analyses to understand harmful algal bloom dynamics, domoic acid biosynthesis, and the functional seascape of marine microbes. He also develops molecular tools such as digital PCR assays and engineered diatom pathways to enhance production of valuable polyunsaturated fatty acids.

faculty.research_interests_enriched: Daniel Cayan investigates climate variability and its hydrologic consequences across the western United States, focusing on drought, sea‑level rise, atmospheric rivers, and their links to flood damage and wildfire risk. faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Evan’s research centers on the dynamics of atmospheric mineral dust and its interactions with climate systems, including its role in radiative forcing, cloud processes, and tropical cyclone activity. faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Helen Fricker’s research centers on the dynamics of the Antarctic and Greenland ice sheets, with a particular emphasis on subglacial hydrology, ice‑ocean interaction, and mass‑balance processes. recent_publications.abstract: Autonomous Lagrangian Circulation Explorer floats recorded temperatures in depths between 700 and 1100 meters in the Southern Ocean throughout the 1990s. These temperature records are systematically warmer than earlier hydrographic temperature measurements from the region, suggesting that mid-depth Southern Ocean temperatures have risen 0.17 degrees C between the 1950s and the 1980s. faculty.research_interests: Atmospheric CO2 measurements, carbon cycle, climate change, Keeling Curve, oxygen measurements
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Ralph Keeling’s research centers on high‑precision atmospheric measurements of CO₂, O₂/N₂, Ar/N₂, He/N₂ and related isotopes to quantify the global carbon cycle and climate change feedbacks. He maintains long‑term flask and airborne time‑series networks in the Arctic, Southern Ocean, and urban environments, and integrates these data with model analyses to assess ocean carbon uptake, deoxygenation, and the impacts of events such as COVID‑19 and ENSO. His work also links atmospheric trace‑gas records to terrestrial ecosystem changes across Arctic‑Boreal regions.

faculty.research_interests: Deep-sea ecology, benthic ecology, oxygen minimum zones, hydrothermal vents, methane seeps
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Lisa Levin investigates the ecology and biogeochemistry of deep‑sea benthic habitats, with particular emphasis on methane seeps, hydrothermal vents, and oxygen‑minimum zones. Her work integrates field experiments using submersibles and ROVs with laboratory physiological and geochemical analyses to understand how extreme chemical environments shape chemosynthetic communities, larval development, and connectivity across continental margins. She also explores the impacts of ocean warming, deoxygenation, and acidification on marine larvae and the broader carbon cycle, linking modern observations to paleo‑ecological contexts such as the Cambrian radiation.

faculty.research_interests_enriched: Professor Stuart Sandin investigates the ecology and conservation of coral reef ecosystems, integrating community ecology, fisheries science, and large‑area imaging to understand and enhance reef resilience. His work spans field surveys of Pacific reef fish and seamount habitats, molecular studies of coral‑associated microbes, and the development of citizen‑driven restoration tools such as the 100 Island Challenge. faculty.research_interests_enriched: Yui Takeshita investigates how ocean acidification and carbon cycling affect coastal and reef ecosystems, with a focus on the biogeochemical processes that regulate seawater chemistry. faculty.research_interests: Physical oceanography, ocean circulation, water masses, thermohaline circulation, climate
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Lynne Talley’s research centers on physical oceanography with an emphasis on ocean circulation, water‑mass transformation, and thermohaline processes that regulate Earth’s climate and biogeochemical cycles. She investigates the dynamics of key water masses such as Antarctic Intermediate Water, Eighteen‑Degree Water, and Ross Gyre waters, and their role in heat, freshwater, and carbon transport, especially in the Southern Ocean and other major basins. Her work integrates extensive observational programs—including GO‑SHIP repeat hydrography, Core‑Argo float deployments, and tracer measurements—with numerical modeling and state‑estimate analyses to quantify climate‑relevant oceanic variability.

faculty.research_interests_enriched: David Victor investigates the design and effectiveness of climate and energy policies at the international and national levels, focusing on pathways to deep decarbonization and the governance of emerging mitigation technologies. His work spans the analysis of policy sequencing, climate finance, and regulatory mechanisms such as naming‑and‑shaming, with applications to sectors including aviation, fuel‑cell vehicles, ocean alkalinity, and bio‑enhanced crops. By integrating economic modeling, expert elicitation, and technology assessment, he seeks to align scientific innovation with actionable climate policy.

recent_publications.abstract: The El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO) is a key ocean-atmospheric mode that affects global weather and climate through atmospheric teleconnections. recent_publications.abstract: The East Asian summer monsoon (EASM) supplies vital rainfall for over one billion people. faculty.title: Professor Emeritus
faculty.research_interests: Geophysics, gravity measurements, seafloor geodesy, optical fiber sensors
faculty.research_interests_enriched: Mark Zumberge develops and applies advanced optical‑fiber and GNSS‑acoustic sensing technologies to achieve high‑precision seafloor geodesy. His work focuses on measuring crustal deformation, slow‑slip events, volcanic flank movement, and subduction‑zone tsunami generation, often through autonomous ocean‑observatory nodes and wave‑glider platforms. He integrates sensor design, distributed acoustic sensing, and gravity measurements to improve earthquake early‑warning and hazard assessment in marine environments.
